paragraphs.body: In the article, “Cheerleading: A prevalence of catastrophic injuries” Brittany Bowyer argues that cheerleading is one of the most dangerous sports. She explains her point by developing several important ideas. First, Bowyer addresses the high risk of severe injuries that can come with be a part of this sport. She points out that “The sport was responsible for 65 percent of direct catastrophic injuries to female high school athletes during a 27-year period.” according to the American Academy of Pediatrics.  Next, she emphasizes the personal and long-lasting impacts that these injuries have on the athletes. She shows us this by interviewing many current, former cheerleaders and their parents on the injuries that have happened to them and their children. Finally, Bowyer touches on the lack of recognition and safety of the sport. Many people don’t recognize cheerleading as being a contact sport, but Bowyer shows us that it is in fact just that. Here is a quote from a former cheerleader that bowyer interviewed “My X-rays showed that I had broken three vertebrae in my neck, and I was actually one vertebra away from being paralyzed from the waist down.” If that doesn’t show you that cheerleading is a contact sport, I don’t know what could. In my opinion bowyer’s main point is to raise awareness for the lack of protection and awareness that cheerleading gets.

annotations.text: In the article, “Cheerleading: A prevalence of catastrophic injuries” Brittany Bowyer argues that cheerleading is one of the most dangerous sports.
annotations.comment: Nice signal verbs and writing moves throughout this paragraph!

paragraphs.body: In this article Bowyer give specific examples of incidents involving competitive cheerleaders. Most common of which being concussions. She also explains how cheerleaders need to be very in shape to be able to perform their rigorous routines. Bowyer believes that cheer should be considered a real sport and wants to bring more attention to the safety when performing. In Bowyer’s research she found that “from the National Center for Catastrophic Sports Injury Research at the University of North Carolina that concluded cheerleading accounted for 65.2 percent of all catastrophic injuries in youth sports.” Putting this research in her article Bowyer shows that cheerleading is more dangerous than most sports. She argues that cheerleading should be taken seriously and challenges the reputation of cheerleading not being a sport.

paragraphs.body: From a young age I was always doing cartwheels down a hill and summersaulting in my room. After watching me do this for years my mom had the bright idea to enroll me into a gymnastics program. I immediately loved it. Eventually I outgrew it, so I did decide to quit. At that time, I was in the eighth grade, getting ready for high school. I needed something to do and that’s when my mom suggested cheerleading. It was the perfect alterative. I already knew how to do all the tumbling from gymnastics and how hard could everything else be? I couldn’t be more wrong.

annotations.text: From a young age I was always doing cartwheels down a hill and summersaulting in my room.
annotations.comment: Nice shift. Great voice and details :)

paragraphs.body: In the article “Cheerleading: A prevalence of catastrophic injuries” Bowler highlights the dangers in cheerleading. Which goes along with cheerleading being underappreciated by not having any to no safety precautions. In my own beliefs the reason for this is because of the reputation cheer gets for being an unserious extracurricular activity. When in fact it’s the opposite. I was a part of a back-to-back state and a 2024 WPIAL championship winning team, so I feel qualified enough to speak on the challenges in this sport. I’d have practice every Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday and have a game on Friday then an endurance training on Sunday. Having to practice that much came with injuries happening through the whole team frequently. The most common being sprained ankles. By the end of the season every flyer on the team had at an ancle brace on. There was one girl who fractured her back, another who had a sprained wrist, a third that who had to get hip surgery. (Which had to be postponed till the day after our state competition) and all this was due to the skills we practice and perform. In Bowyers article she shares a story of a former cheerleader. “When Alexa first began cheering, she was a flyer. Following a growth spurt, she became a base. As her stunt group was in the process of learning a new skill, Alexa was kicked in the left temple. She didn’t know that this would be the first of three concussions that would eventually lead to her retirement from cheer.” By adding this story Bowyer is showing us how easy it is to get seriously injured. I also had something similar happen to me. In the tenth grade my base slipped from under me and my knee landed right in her face. Immediately her lip started gushing blood. Later that night she had to get two stiches in her lip. That was the first time I realized the importance of safety and knowing what to do when falling from a stunt.

annotations.text: reputation cheer gets for being an unserious extracurricular activity.

paragraphs.body: I want to make it clear that I’m talking about competitive cheer, not game day. What’s the difference? Game day is jumping around and cheering for a sport, maybe a stunt here and there, which is what most people think when talking about cheer but generally doesn’t require much athleticism. Competitive cheer is less common. I look at it as a sport. Physically competing skills against other teams. I always feel the need to elaborate on the difference because of the reputation that cheerleading gets as an unimportant and flippant extracurricular activity. For my economics class last year all the students had to split up into groups and interview people with random questions. A popular question was is cheer a sport? Most of the people just smirked and quickly said no. Hearing answers like those didn’t get me upset or mad.

annotations.text: I want to make it clear that I’m talking about competitive cheer, not game day. What’s the difference?
annotations.comment: This is a great move to define your terms. Nice. But is this paragraph in the "right" order? Should it come early in your essay so that your reader is already familiar with the differences?

paragraphs.body: I understand their point of view. In the column cheerleading isn’t a sport, it’s an Olympic sport, Zach Edmondson himself writes “If the cheerleaders are simply shouting and throwing their arms around every time, I look at them during football games, then I wouldn’t think what they do is athletic. Some school’s cheer squads perform routines that would make you think there was another team competing with them though, and that is hard for me to discredit as something less than a sport.” Basically, Edmondson is saying that cheerleading squads perform routines that are so impressive it’s hard to call that not a sport.
